title: Needle-felted fox
subtitle: A sitting fox figure in rust-orange merino with white chest and black leg markings
excerpt: Needle-felt a compact sitting fox in rust-orange merino, with a white chest bib, black-tipped legs, and a bushy white-tipped tail. The sitting pose is built from two joined elements: a round body and a tapered tail attached at an angle. The colour contrasts make it a useful project for learning how to work clean colour boundaries in needle felting.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted fox figures are among the most commonly made British wildlife subjects. The sitting-fox form with tail as a separate element is a standard guild construction method.

glossaryTerms.definition: The edge between two different-coloured sections of a needle-felted piece. A clean colour boundary is achieved by needling each colour in from its own side without crossing the edge; a blended boundary is achieved by thinning both colours to wisps at the join.
The method of joining a separately felted tail piece to a body by laying the joining end of the tail against the body and needling through both layers, then blending roving over the join to hide it.

content.text: The sitting fox consists of a rounded body about 7 cm tall, a tapered tail about 8 cm long, and surface markings at the chest, legs, and tail tip. The key skill is working clean
between the orange body and the white chest and the black leg socks. The
method joins the tail at an angle to suggest it curling around the seated fox.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

items.name: Merino roving, black (leg socks, ear tips)

content.text: Step 1: Build the body and head

Take 16 g of rust-orange roving and compact it into an egg shape about 7 cm tall on the mat with a size 38 needle. Work from the bottom first, then ease the upper section into a slightly pointed head area by concentrating strokes there. The sitting body is rounder at the base and tapers slightly toward the head.

Roll 6 g of rust-orange roving into a tapered sausage about 8 cm long, wider at the base and narrowing to a point. Needle it firm on the mat, keeping the taper. Then take 2 g of white roving and needle it over the last 2 cm of the narrow end to form the white tail tip.

Hold the wide end of the tail against the lower-back-right of the body at a slight downward angle, as though the tail is curling around the fox's right side. Needle through both layers at the join to secure. Add a thin layer of rust-orange roving over the join and needle it smooth to hide the seam.

Use the size 40 fine needle. Needle a thin veil of white roving over the lower front of the body to form the chest bib, working from the edge inward for a soft boundary. Add black roving at the lower front legs (the sock markings) and at the ear-tip points. Use very small amounts of roving for these details.

items.problem: The white chest bleeds into the orange body
items.cause: Too much white roving applied, or strokes crossed the colour boundary
items.fix: Use a very thin veil of white and needle from the white area inward, never from the orange side toward the white.

items.cause: The join was not needled through both layers deeply enough
items.fix: Work additional strokes through the base of the tail straight into the body, penetrating both layers fully.

items.problem: The fox does not sit upright without support
items.cause: The base is rounded rather than flat
items.fix: Press the base of the seated body against the foam mat and needle downward strokes to flatten it slightly before attaching the tail.
